title: employees table has zero explicit indexes despite being filtered by departmentId/employmentStatus/managerEmployeeId on nearly every query
page: Employees list, Departments, Attendance, Leave, Payroll (any screen scoped by employee/department)
description: packages/db/src/schema/tenant/employees.ts defines the employees table with no index()/uniqueIndex() calls at all. This table is the most heavily filtered/joined table in the app: employee.ts filters on departmentId and employmentStatus and sorts by fullName/hireDate/createdAt (apps/web/trpc/routers/employee.ts:18-37); attendance.ts filters employees by departmentId (line 459) and joins via managerEmployeeId for org-tree walks; leave.ts filters active employees by employmentStatus (line 337); departments.ts joins on headEmployeeId. Postgres does not auto-index foreign-key columns (unlike the primary key), so every one of these WHERE/JOIN clauses does a sequential scan once tenant employee counts grow past a few hundred rows. Only 7 of 33 schema files (packages/db/src/schema/**) define any explicit index at all; employees.ts and leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts are all in the no-index list.
expectedBehavior: Foreign-key / frequently-filtered columns (departmentId, employmentStatus, managerEmployeeId) should have explicit btree indexes so list/report queries scale with tenant size.
actualBehavior: No index definitions exist on the employees table or on most other tenant tables that are filtered by employeeId/departmentId in routers.

evidence.snippet: export const employees = pgTable("employees", { id: uuid(...).primaryKey(), departmentId: uuid(...).references(() => departments.id, ...), managerEmployeeId: uuid(...), ... }); // no index()/uniqueIndex() anywhere in file

reproduction: Static evidence only — grep packages/db/src/schema for `index(`: only attendance.ts, shift-related and 6 other files define indexes; employees.ts, leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts have none.
technicalRisk: Sequential scans on core tenant tables as employee/leave/expense/document/payslip counts grow; query latency degrades non-linearly with tenant size.
businessRisk: Slower dashboards and reports for larger customer tenants; risk grows directly with the company's success (more employees = slower app), which is a bad first impression during demos/scale-up.
recommendation: Add btree indexes on employees.departmentId, employees.employmentStatus, employees.managerEmployeeId, and the employeeId foreign key on leave_requests/expenses/documents/payslips/notifications; regenerate a Drizzle migration (pnpm db:generate) rather than hand-editing SQL.
workaround: None at scale currently deployed (demo tenant is small so this is not yet visible in production).

title: attendance.getSubtree fetches every employee in the tenant on every call instead of the requested org subtree
page: Attendance guide map (org subtree with last-known GPS location)
description: The comment above the query says "Recursive CTE to get all employees under rootEmployeeId" but the actual Drizzle call has no `where` clause and no `limit`, so it loads the entire employees table (with department join) for the tenant on every request, then filters/builds the org tree in application memory (apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts:722-758). A second unbounded findMany over attendanceRecords (with: {}) then follows for every employee in that full result set.
expectedBehavior: Only employees within the requested rootEmployeeId's subtree (and their latest attendance record) should be fetched from the database, ideally via a recursive CTE or a scoped WHERE clause.
actualBehavior: ctx.db.query.employees.findMany({ with: { department: true } }) has no where/limit — it is a full tenant-wide employee scan on every guide-map request.
reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts lines 710-780 (getSubtree procedure) — no where clause is ever applied to the employees.findMany call before the in-memory tree/filter logic runs.
technicalRisk: Every guide-map page load/refresh does an O(all tenant employees) fetch + O(n) in-memory tree walk regardless of how small the requested subtree is; scales poorly for larger tenants and increases DB load under concurrent map views.
businessRisk: Slower/laggy GPS map for larger customers; wasted DB read capacity that could throttle other tenants sharing the database.
recommendation: Replace the full-table findMany with a recursive CTE (raw SQL via Drizzle's sql`` helper) or an iterative BFS that only expands children of rootEmployeeId, and add a limit as a safety cap.
workaround: None; acceptable today only because demo/pilot tenants are small.

title: leave.runAccrual issues one sequential DB write per employee×leaveType combination instead of a bulk operation
description: The `runAccrual` mutation computes accrual results for every active employee × leave type in memory, then loops over the results issuing one `await ctx.db.insert(...)` or `await ctx.db.update(...)` per row sequentially inside a for-loop, rather than batching the writes.
expectedBehavior: Bulk insert/update (e.g. a single multi-row insert with onConflict upsert, or Promise.all with a bounded concurrency) so the accrual run scales sub-linearly with employee count.
actualBehavior: for (const result of results) { await ctx.db.insert(...) } / await ctx.db.update(...) — strictly sequential, one round trip per employee per leave type.
technicalRisk: For a tenant with N active employees and M leave types, this is up to N×M sequential DB round trips inside one HTTP-triggered mutation; risk of request timeout or long-held DB connections during monthly/annual cron runs as tenants grow.
businessRisk: Cron job could start timing out or slowing down as customer headcount grows, silently delaying leave balance updates.
recommendation: Batch inserts with a single multi-row INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, or chunk the loop with Promise.all in small batches (e.g. 20 at a time) to reduce round trips while bounding connection usage.

title: pnpm audit: drizzle-orm pinned below the patched version for a high-severity SQL-injection advisory
description: `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` reports one high-severity advisory: "Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers" (GHSA-gpj5-g38j-94v9), affecting drizzle-orm <0.45.2. packages/db/package.json pins "drizzle-orm": "^0.38.0" and the resolved version is 0.38.4 (12 dependency paths). `pnpm audit` (no level filter) additionally reports 4 moderate advisories: esbuild <=0.24.2 (dev-server request/response exposure, via drizzle-kit's esbuild-kit chain), next-auth 5.0.0-beta.25 (email misdelivery, patched at beta.30), and postcss <8.5.10 (XSS via unescaped </style> in stringify output, pulled in via Next.js 16.2.10).
expectedBehavior: Dependencies with known high-severity advisories should be upgraded to patched versions promptly, especially the ORM used for every tenant-scoped query.
actualBehavior: drizzle-orm is pinned to ^0.38.0 (resolves to 0.38.4), below the 0.45.2 patched version.

evidence.snippet: high | Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers | Vulnerable versions <0.45.2 | Patched versions >=0.45.2 | GHSA-gpj5-g38j-94v9

reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm audit --audit-level=high  (also: pnpm audit, for the 4 additional moderate findings)
technicalRisk: If any code path builds SQL identifiers (table/column names) from less-trusted input while on the vulnerable drizzle-orm version, injection is possible; even if no such path currently exists, staying on a flagged version is a standing liability that must be re-checked with every new query added.
businessRisk: A published CVE against the core data-access library of an HR/payroll SaaS handling PII and salary data is a due-diligence red flag for enterprise customers and auditors.
recommendation: Upgrade drizzle-orm to >=0.45.2 (check for breaking changes against drizzle-kit and schema code), upgrade next-auth to >=5.0.0-beta.30, and let drizzle-kit pull in a patched esbuild-kit/esbuild chain; postcss will resolve once Next.js patches its bundled dependency.
workaround: None; not yet exploited but exposure is real.

title: No timeout or retry on any external HTTP call (Qiwa gov API, Gemini, Claude)
page: AI assistant chat, Qiwa sync features
description: Every external fetch() call in the Qiwa client (packages/qiwa/src/index.ts, 8 call sites: auth token, employees, contracts, contract status, health check) and both LLM providers (packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts:65, packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts:56) is a bare `await fetch(...)` with no AbortController/signal timeout and no retry/backoff logic.
expectedBehavior: External calls to third-party/government APIs and LLM providers should have a bounded timeout (e.g. 10-30s via AbortController) and at least one retry for transient network errors, so a slow upstream cannot hang a user-facing request indefinitely.
actualBehavior: fetch() calls have no `signal`/timeout option anywhere in the qiwa or llm packages; a hung upstream connection will hang the calling tRPC procedure until the platform's own request timeout (if any) kicks in.

evidence.snippet: response = await fetch(url, { method: "POST", headers: {...}, body: JSON.stringify({...}) });

reproduction: grep -n "fetch(\|timeout\|AbortController\|retry" packages/qiwa/src/index.ts packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts — no timeout/retry keyword matches at any call site.
technicalRisk: A slow or hanging Qiwa/Gemini/Claude endpoint ties up a server-side request (and DB connection, if the procedure holds one) for however long the runtime allows before killing it; no graceful degradation for transient failures.
businessRisk: User-facing AI chat or Qiwa sync actions could appear to hang rather than fail fast with a retry or clear error, hurting perceived reliability during demos and real usage.
recommendation: Add an AbortController-based timeout (e.g. 15s for LLM, 20s for Qiwa) to every external fetch, and a single retry with backoff for network-level failures (not for 4xx application errors).
workaround: The AI chat path already has a try/catch fallback to a stub reply for context-fetching, which limits blast radius for that one call; the main completion call itself has no fallback.

title: pnpm lint fails with 372 errors (almost entirely no-explicit-any / no-non-null-assertion) confined to the web app
description: `pnpm lint` fails (turbo reports @hrms-app/web#lint exited 1). All 16 other packages (auth, config, date, db, demo, documents, email, leave, llm, payroll, qiwa, queue, ui, validators, etc.) lint clean. Only apps/web fails, with 387 problems (372 errors, 15 warnings) across ~84 files. The dominant rule is @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any (the large majority of the 372 errors — 295 raw `: any`/`as any` occurrences were found across apps/web by static grep), followed by @typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion (~30+ occurrences, e.g. attendance/me/page.tsx, payroll pages, leave.ts, invite.ts) and a smaller number of no-unused-vars.
expectedBehavior: `pnpm lint` should exit 0 across all packages, per the project's own ESLint config which forbids `any` and non-null assertions.
technicalRisk: Widespread `any` erases type safety exactly where drizzle-orm's inferred query/relation types would otherwise catch schema-mismatch bugs at compile time; non-null assertions (`!`) can throw at runtime if the assumption is ever wrong.
businessRisk: A failing lint gate on every non-trivial page means CI/lint is effectively not enforced for apps/web today, letting type-safety erosion compound silently.
recommendation: Triage by rule: replace `any` in router callback signatures (e.g. attendance.ts:60-61, department.ts:11, leave.ts:82) with the inferred Drizzle relation types; replace non-null assertions with explicit null checks or narrow the type upstream. Given the volume, tackle incrementally per-router rather than one large PR.

title: Zero automated tests for the two largest business-logic routers: retention.ts (1611 lines) and recruitment.ts (812 lines)
page: Retention (goals, reviews, skills, succession, engagement, rewards, compensation), Recruitment (jobs, candidates, applications, interviews, offers, onboarding, referrals, background/reference checks)
description: The vitest run (152 tests passed across 26 suite files) contains no test file for apps/web/trpc/routers/retention.ts or apps/web/trpc/routers/recruitment.ts. These are the two single largest source files in the repo (1611 and 812 lines respectively) and implement RBAC-scoped CRUD + workflow logic for goals/OKRs, performance reviews, skills/succession, engagement surveys, rewards/compensation, and the full recruitment pipeline (candidates through onboarding). No __tests__ directory reference to `retention` or `recruitment` domain logic exists anywhere in packages/ or apps/web/src/__tests__.
expectedBehavior: Business-logic routers of this size, especially ones gating access by requireCapability/requireRole (payroll:view_company, performance:view_team, etc.), should have integration tests covering at least the RBAC gate and core CRUD/workflow paths.
actualBehavior: No test files reference these routers; coverage is 0% at the unit/integration level for both.

evidence.snippet: 1611 total lines; no corresponding packages/*/__tests__ or apps/web/src/__tests__ file exists for this router
812 total lines; no corresponding test file exists
Test Files  1 failed | 25 passed (26)  — no retention/recruitment suite among the 26

technicalRisk: Regressions in RBAC scoping (e.g. perfView/compView capability gates), pagination, or workflow state transitions in these two routers would not be caught by the test suite before reaching production.
businessRisk: These modules touch performance ratings, succession planning, compensation, and the hiring pipeline — errors here are visible to managers and candidates and could leak data across the department/company boundary without a test catching it.
recommendation: Add integration tests per sub-router (goal, reviewCycle, skill, succession, engagement, reward, compensation for retention; job/candidate/application/interview/offer/onboarding/referral for recruitment) covering: RBAC gate rejection for unauthorized roles, pagination correctness, and at least one full create->read->update happy path per entity.

title: Tenant-isolation regression tests are mostly skipped, not executing
description: The vitest run shows packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-isolation.test.ts as "4 tests | 3 skipped" and packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-manager.test.ts as "5 tests | 4 skipped" — i.e. the majority of the tests specifically named for verifying cross-tenant data isolation are not actually executing in the current run (likely gated behind a live-DB precondition, consistent with the known-broken local DATABASE_URL noted in project memory).
expectedBehavior: Tenant-isolation tests are the most important regression guard in a multi-tenant SaaS (a prior RBAC audit already scored this area 90/100 after remediation) and should run on every CI build, not be silently skipped.
actualBehavior: 7 of 9 combined tests across the two tenant-isolation-related suites are skipped in this run.
reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm test — observe the skipped counts in the summary line for these two files.
technicalRisk: If these skips are due to a missing live-DB connection (consistent with the local DATABASE_URL being broken per project memory), the same skip condition may also apply in CI unless CI provisions a real Postgres instance — meaning the most safety-critical tests in the repo may not be gating merges.
businessRisk: A regression that leaks data across tenants (the single worst failure mode for this SaaS) could ship without the dedicated tests for it ever running.
recommendation: Confirm whether CI provisions a real DATABASE_URL so these suites run un-skipped there; if the skip is environment-conditional, add a CI-only Postgres service (e.g. via GitHub Actions services:) so tenant isolation is verified on every PR, not just locally when a DB happens to be available.
workaround: Tests exist and presumably pass when a DB is available; this is a coverage-execution gap, not a missing-test gap.

title: Several tRPC routers with real business logic have no dedicated unit/integration tests (attendance, expense, compliance, department, settlement, notification)
module: Attendance / Expense / Compliance / Department / Settlement / Notification
page: Attendance, Expenses, Compliance, Departments, Settlements, Notifications
description: apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts (788 lines: shifts, shift assignments, punches, exceptions, monthly reports, GPS subtree) has no matching unit/integration test file; it is only touched indirectly by two Playwright UI specs (attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts), which test rendering, not the underlying query/business logic (overtime calc, late/absence classification, subtree building). expense.ts, compliance.ts, department.ts, settlement.ts, and notification.ts routers similarly have no dedicated test file.
expectedBehavior: Routers implementing calculations (overtime, late minutes) or RBAC-scoped visibility rules (expense.ts's SEC-004 remediation for pending-approval visibility) should have logic-level tests independent of the UI.
actualBehavior: Only e2e/UI-level coverage exists for attendance; no coverage at all for expense/compliance/department/settlement/notification routers.

evidence.snippet: 788 lines; no packages/*/__tests__ or apps/web/src/__tests__ file references attendance business logic
// SESSION employee, never a client-supplied id (SEC-004 — was an IDOR). — this RBAC-sensitive logic has no regression test

reproduction: find . -name '*.test.ts' | grep -iE 'attendance|expense|compliance|department|settlement|notification' — no matches beyond leave/payroll's own attendance-adjacent overtime tests in packages/payroll.
technicalRisk: Regressions in overtime/late calculation or RBAC-scoped expense visibility (previously an IDOR, per the code comment) would only be caught by manual QA or production incidents, not CI.
businessRisk: Payroll-adjacent overtime numbers and expense approval visibility are financially and legally sensitive (Saudi labour law); untested logic here carries compliance risk.
recommendation: Prioritize a test for expense.ts's role-scoped visibility (given it references a prior IDOR fix) and attendance.ts's overtime/late calculation before adding new features to these routers.

title: Only 3 Playwright e2e specs exist; no end-to-end coverage of payroll run, leave approval, or recruitment pipeline
description: apps/web/e2e/ contains exactly 3 Playwright specs: auth.spec.ts, attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts (plus a non-test production-performance.mjs script). There is no e2e coverage for payroll run creation/approval, leave request/approval, employee onboarding, or the recruitment pipeline — all multi-step, high-value user flows.
expectedBehavior: Critical multi-step flows (per CLAUDE.md's own testing rule requiring E2E for critical flows) should have at least a smoke-level Playwright spec.
actualBehavior: E2E coverage is limited to auth and two attendance screens.

evidence.snippet: attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts, auth.spec.ts, production-performance.mjs — 3 spec files total

technicalRisk: UI regressions in payroll/leave/recruitment flows would not be caught by any automated browser test.
businessRisk: Lower confidence that a UI refactor doesn't silently break the payroll run or leave approval flow before a release.
recommendation: Add smoke-level Playwright specs for: leave request -> manager approval, payroll run creation -> approval -> payslip view, and recruitment candidate -> offer -> onboarding happy path.
workaround: docs/e2e-status.md may already track this gap — cross-check before duplicating planning.

title: Two router files exceed the project's own 800-line file-size guideline
description: apps/web/trpc/routers/retention.ts is 1611 lines (2x the 800-line max) and recruitment.ts is 812 lines. Both are flat single-file routers covering many unrelated sub-domains (retention.ts alone spans goals, review cycles, reviews, skills, succession planning, engagement surveys, rewards, compensation, and talent reviews).
expectedBehavior: Per the project's coding-style guideline (200-400 lines typical, 800 max), large routers should be split by sub-domain into separate files/modules.
actualBehavior: retention.ts and recruitment.ts remain single monolithic files well past the stated maximum.
reproduction: find apps packages -name '*.ts' -o -name '*.tsx' | xargs wc -l | sort -rn | head
technicalRisk: Large single files slow code review, increase merge-conflict likelihood, and make it harder to reason about which capability gate applies to which section.
businessRisk: Slower feature velocity in the retention/recruitment modules as the team grows.
recommendation: Split retention.ts into per-sub-domain router files (goal.ts, review.ts, skill.ts, succession.ts, engagement.ts, reward.ts, compensation.ts) merged via createTRPCRouter composition, mirroring the pattern already used for top-level routers in trpc/index.ts.
workaround: None needed urgently; purely a maintainability concern.

title: Leading-wildcard LIKE search on employees.fullName cannot use a standard index
description: The employee search filter uses `like(schema.tenant.employees.fullName, \`%${input.search}%\`)` with a leading wildcard, which prevents a standard btree index from being used even if one existed (see QA-001) — Postgres would need a trigram (pg_trgm) or full-text index to serve this efficiently.
expectedBehavior: Name search should use an index-friendly strategy (trigram index, or a prefix-only search) once tenant employee counts grow large.
actualBehavior: Leading-wildcard LIKE forces a full scan of the filtered result set for the search term.
technicalRisk: Search-by-name degrades linearly with tenant employee count; combined with QA-001 (no index at all on employees), this compounds.
businessRisk: Minor at current scale; would surface as a slow employee search for large enterprise tenants.
recommendation: Add a pg_trgm GIN index on employees.full_name (CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm; CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (full_name gin_trgm_ops)) if/when tenant sizes grow past a few thousand employees.
